item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated this page again. I am planning to write some more stuff with Dr. Da Costa and the Gash Group supertech, so this will serve as his first major discovery and the beginning of his rise to Foundation stardom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could you take another look? I've cleaned up some unneeded blackboxing and I think the article overall makes more sense now.

Thanks a lot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a big problem, thanks for pointing it out! I've switched to using 100 light-picoseconds, which is really close to 3cm and a fundamental universal fact, rather than a derived unit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed! Thanks for the advice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I solved both of these concerns by editing the advice to just say "don't store them in a straight line from each other" and to use 100 light-picoseconds as the distance, which is _extremely_ close to 3 centimeters and is a fundamental fact of physics, not a derived unit.

Thanks for the advice!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way worse scips than this one are in the 40's or 50's.

It's not bad enough to merit a 6, so I'll make it a 7. But feel free to change it to make it more appealing to the masses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty great idea buried under a bunch of first series style junk, but not the good first series style junk. Normally I would novote and move on, but I agree with folks that this could use a heavy rewrite to go from "okay" to "spectacular". -1 to help push things toward said rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also:
"Following incident SCP-2568-C, if multiple instances are contained within the same room, they are to be spaced apart at distances which are not multiples of 5 centimeters."

In what measuring scale? Metric? Imperial? Either? What about some very niche, local scales used by ethnically distinct locals who keep to their ways? What about some ancient measuring scales?
Not wanting to sound harsh - but that description is vague, because there are so many measurement scales and units that if you look long enough, eventually every possible distance can be "translated" into a multiple of five in some other measurement scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I overall like this, but come to think about it: aren't these fundamentally impossible to have their position modulo 5cm, changed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing that jars with me is this: the creators were an ancient civilisation, yet the units of measurement (and in their movement) appear to be metric.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a bit miffed that this article isn't doing too well, to be honest. I really enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These blackboxes are awful.

Blackboxing why?
– hide block

The sudden duplication of mass and collision of particles within the strong magnetic field caused ██████ to ████ with the ████ of the upper instance, creating ████ and a massive explosion which destroyed the entire locker area.

All of this is INCREDIBLY INTERESTING AND RELEVANT, and there's no real reason to expunge it at all, much less to use blackboxing for it.

As the standard lockers at Facility ██ are █5 centimeters

Not as big a deal, but there's really no reason to redact the size of the lockers, and that's just distracting. Facility blackbox is fine.

member of MTF ████ during breakfast

There doesn't seem to be any reason to blackbox the MTF name, so this is again just distracting.

British Royal ███████ and the ███████████ Institute, who determined that it was almost certainly a game board and that it was at least 1███ years old.

Blackbox the entire thing or don't bother, I think. Distracting to have PART of these names blackboxed. Also, blackboxing everything but the 1 in a four digit number is also distracting. Better to just say 'at least one thousand years old'.

SCP-2568 had passed through his skull and ██████ ██ ████, killing him instantly.

No reason to blackbox this. It's obvious what it's saying, and there's no real purpose to redacting it, so it's doubly distracting.

Apart from that, the scientist notes are pretty hamfisted and don't contribute much, once again distracting from other aspects of the article. The O-5 response feels pretty off too, but I'm not sure why I think that, so big grain of salt on that one.

That said, I rather do like the core idea. Weird teleporty game pieces from probably-Egypt? Alright, that's neat and has some really weird potential. I'm left with one more biggish point of concern though - how do these things get moved IN GENERAL, for example to even get them into containment? Do they keep teleporting when, say, lifted on the game board? If you put them in a box, can you move the box, or do they teleport out? These kinds of questions are things the people who originally MADE them would have to answer as well, so it kinda feels like a huge deal that it feels so poorly addressed in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the foundation clearly knows where it passed through, since examined the body and all. It shouldn't be classified, as it's just a body part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a very weird thing to censor

But why? In the future, please be more descriptive.
I'd recommend replacing it with a good ol' REDACTED or DATA EXPUNGED.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with all of this. This is a relatively intriguing base idea, hampered by trite and uninteresting execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They kind of behave like quantum objects only on the wrong scale.

But what I wanted to say is:
If the objects can only be moved in 5 cm increments then surely it is impossible to space them at distances that are not multiples of 5cm as dictated by the containment?
If I understand correctly they are locked into a 5 cm x 5 cm universal grid and there is nothing the foundation can do about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. This needs a lot of cleanup.
- The dialogue between the doctors didn't feel natural and definitely wasn't worked into the document in any realistic way (i.e., not chucking signed notes willy nilly through an official report).
- The bit about it being locked to the surface of the Earth and subsequent elaboration definitely felt like it was covering up a problem after the fact and not worked in. It might not have been such a problem of the dialogue or countering theories had been explored in some interesting way, but they were just kinda there.
- The tone was farther into tongue in cheek territory than it could sustain (see the unrealistic dialogue problem).
- You've expunged a ton of stuff that could've given this some more depth.
- Finally, making these things blow up (causing a breach) and then kill a D-Class in testing are probably the most boring things you could possibly have done with them.

The virtual velocity idea is decent, but you've ignored a lot of opportunities for deeper human storytelling in favor of really old, boring site tropes. There's the theory conflict between the researchers, which could be interesting if presented more coherently. You get a bit into resource and containment problems, which is alright. There's the question of who was in the tomb, and just why they or their society would be interested enough in a game where the pieces can only move the precise board distance that they'd be willing to break physics. Or is that normal for them? That kind of thing.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it was discovered that the final instance of SCP-2568 had passed through his skull and ██████ ██ ████, killing him instantly.

That's a very weird thing to censor.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Fixed to use https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Stainless_steel_1.4034_X46Cr13_bearing_ball_60mm_G100_removed_camera.jpg

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page. If the images are not wholly your own, please post the image's source as a link (or links) in reply to this post and edit it into your first post in this discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just kinda don't get it, I suppose. The ramifications of the physicals properties of these things aren't really clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What it needs is to be stripped back a little. The interesting bit is the object; focus on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note by Dr. M█████: This is, of course, relative to the planet Earth. If not, each of these things would be constantly hopping about and flashing as the planet turns and moves through space. I'd love to get one of these out into the Moon's gravity well and see what happens!

Note by O5-█: Request denied. That's a little more expense than we can stomach for a Safe at this time.

I assume you're not aware of the existence of SCP-120

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still in neutral vote territory on this one. While I think you've taken some good steps on developing the article since its original draft phase, I think you've basically taken a lot of steps with the article, moving it in several directions at once, some of which are good, but most of which end up bogging the thing down. The incident log is, in particular, pretty unnecessary, because it's just tacking on a way in which this thing is dangerous. The D-Class experimentation is the same way; I have zero reason to care that this guy randomly dies when using the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, simple but interesting. I like the idea of an ancient culture that was strangely advanced in certain facets of science. This could be the beginning of a hub in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I see what you mean. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of games kill people when pieces are misused, and sometimes even during legal play; see archery and riflery, jousting, football, et cetera.

I'm sorry it wasn't interesting; is it that the idea itself is uninteresting, or is there something wrong with the way I wrote it up? Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a little confusing, and not particularly interesting, save that these were made for some kind of ancient game. But what good is a game that can kill someone when they touch the pieces?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Each is, according to every test performed so far, entirely normal except for two anomalous properties: they move only in increments of 5 centimeters along their axes and rotate only in increments of 45 degrees around their axes."

Maybe change to: "instances of SCP-2568 appear to have no anomalous properties with the exception that they are only capable of movement within increments of 5 centimeters along their axes and rotate only in increments of 45 degrees around their axes."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm happy to change it but, I don't really see what you take issue with. Could you please elaborate a bit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the beginning description paragraph could be rephrased to be a little more clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good point. I'll add that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it can only move in increments of 5, then riddle me this:

Why are these things not constantly strobing like lights at a Rave when you consider…

  • The Earth rotates at a speed of 1,600 km/h
  • The Earth orbits the sun at a speed of 108,000 km/h
  • The Sun orbits within the Milky Way at a speed of 720,000 km/h
  • The Galaxy moves at a speed of 2.1 million km/h

When I was faced with a similar dilemma, I simply added a footnote that said "Relative to immediate surroundings", though that may not work in this case. Maybe "Relative to the Earth" would work in this case?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I take your point; I've gone ahead and added a bit more narrative to the piece. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the premise of this article. I feel like it could use additional development though, you've got a good setup of these two scientists with differing hypothesis and wanting to investigate this tomb further, but then you call it too soon. More information on that would make this article feel more complete, right now what's here is good but feels like only the first third or so of a story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry about that. I guess it's on my mind because I just finished the game, heh.

Anyway, SCPs that muck about with the laws of physics are a favourite of mine, and this one I felt was really well-executed. Deserves the +1 by a long way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, but now I'm going to go look that up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really taking a huge stab in the dark here, but…

+ Show stab in the dark
- Hide stab in the dark

I don't suppose the baseline idea here was influenced by the stasis rune from Breath of the Wild?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Stainless_steel_1.4034_X46Cr13_bearing_ball_60mm_G100_removed_camera.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good. I'm a huge fan of objects what break physics - and the idea of an object with a "refresh rate" that can only perform rectilinear motion is really freaking cool.

+1, easily.
